summary: The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a special meeting zoning workshop with the Zoning Commission, Board of Zoning Appeals, and invited guest Kevin Maloff (who did not attend). Key discussions included the land use plan update progress, a proposed 20% non-residential requirement for PDM (Planned Development Mixed-use) rezonings with a 60% residential build-out trigger, removal of the 2,000 square foot aggregate cap on accessory structures, revising accessory dwelling unit (ADU) square footage maximums, and potential adoption of the International Property Maintenance Code (IPMC).
decisions: Motion to adopt the agenda passed unanimously
Motion to adjourn the special meeting at 7:13 PM passed unanimously
action_items: Director Langenheim to prepare draft resolution language for 20% non-residential PDM requirement with 60% residential trigger for further review
Director Langenheim to crunch numbers and prepare scenarios on ADU square footage percentages for a future workshop
Director Langenheim to purchase digital and physical copies of the International Property Maintenance Code (~$80) for distribution to BZA, Zoning Commission, and Trustees for review
Director Langenheim to get clarification from prosecutor's office on whether IPMC fine appeals go to court or BZA
Director Langenheim to coordinate with Will Menz on potential digital complaint submission system for the website
Drew DeMarsh to help facilitate public land use plan workshop on March 3rd
Staff to promote the land use plan survey on website and Facebook for maximum public input
key_amounts: $80 approximate cost for one physical and one digital redline copy of the International Property Maintenance Code
20% proposed minimum non-residential requirement for PDM rezonings
60% residential build-out threshold trigger before non-residential development must proceed in PDM projects
40% current residential requirement on PDM developments
2,000 square foot current aggregate maximum for accessory structures proposed for removal
800 square foot current cap on accessory dwelling units proposed for removal
25% current maximum ADU size relative to primary structure
7% lot area maximum for accessory structures
1,400 square foot minimum home size in Goshen Township
$25-$75 typical property maintenance code violation fees for smaller townships
notable_topics: Land use plan update progress and March 3rd public workshop
PDM residential and non-residential requirements - 20% business minimum and 60% residential trigger
PLK Communities Smith Road apartment application as example of PDM misuse for purely residential projects
Removal of 2,000 square foot aggregate cap on accessory structures
Accessory dwelling unit (ADU) square footage maximums and 25% proportional rule inadequacy
International Property Maintenance Code (IPMC) adoption discussion
State Route 28 corridor and downtown overlay district
Housing development pressure and market trends toward high-density residential
Federal government passing rules on increasing housing density
Tiny home communities and current zoning limitations
Eagles Nest mixed-use development phasing requirements
Downtown Goshen revitalization challenges and dilapidated properties
Comparison with neighboring communities: Loveland, Milford, Mason, Westchester, Union Township, Hamilton Township
attendees: Steve (Zoning Commission or BZA member, referenced farm on Woodville)
Bob (Trustee, referenced as Trustee Allen or Hausermann)
Kevin Maloff (invited but did not attend)
notes: This was a zoning workshop rather than a regular business meeting, so no formal legislative actions were taken beyond agenda adoption and adjournment. Kevin Maloff was listed on the agenda but did not attend. Discussion was wide-ranging and conversational, with significant input from zoning commission and BZA members. The federal government reportedly passed rules on increasing housing density the same day as this meeting. Commissioner Batchelor's veterans tiny home project was mentioned as having been rejected by Bethel Township before moving to Felicity. Stan Beck's local hardware store was mentioned as an example of supporting local Goshen businesses. The moratorium on development is expected to end in April 2026, with the land use plan needing to be completed by then, possibly requiring emergency action.
title: BOT Special Meeting 2-17-2026
